description:
A molecular biology researcher has three tab-separated (TSV) tables stored in

/home/user/datasets/genes.tsv  
/home/user/datasets/expression.tsv  
/home/user/datasets/sample_info.tsv  

She wants to reorganise a subset of the columns into two new summary files so she can quickly inspect the expression levels of the first two RNA-seq samples (sample_A and sample_B) next to the gene names, and likewise check the third sample (sample_C) separately.  
You have terminal access (no root) and must do the following exclusively with standard command-line text-processing utilities such as cut and paste (do **not** open a spreadsheet, python, R, etc.):

1. From genes.tsv take only the columns  
   • gene_id (1st column)  
   • gene_name (2nd column)  

2. From expression.tsv take only the columns  
   • sample_A (2nd column)  
   • sample_B (3rd column)  
   • sample_C (4th column)  

3. Horizontally merge (paste) the selected columns so that you end up with two new files inside the same directory (/home/user/datasets):  

   A) /home/user/datasets/summary_ab.tsv containing exactly four columns, in this *left-to-right* order and separated by single TAB characters:  
      gene_id    gene_name    sample_A    sample_B  

   B) /home/user/datasets/summary_c.tsv containing exactly three columns, in this *left-to-right* order and separated by single TAB characters:  
      gene_id    gene_name    sample_C  

   Both files must keep the header row followed by the five data rows (the sets are small enough that no filtering is necessary).  
   IMPORTANT:  
   • Do not reorder the rows; preserve the original row order of genes.tsv.  
   • Do not introduce extra blank lines or spaces; the automated checker will read the files as TAB-delimited.  
   • All numeric expression values must remain exactly as they appear in expression.tsv (no rounding, quoting, or re-formatting).

4. Make a new directory /home/user/research_logs (it does not exist yet) and create the text file /home/user/research_logs/column_extraction.log.  
   Every time you run a command that is part of your cut-and-paste workflow (including mkdir, cut, paste, mv, etc.), append a single line to that log in the following, strictly enforced format:

      [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] the_exact_command_you_ran_here

   Example:

      [2025-01-31 14:07:42] cut -f1,2 /home/user/datasets/genes.tsv > genes_12.tsv

   Rules the checker will enforce:  
   • The timestamp must be in 24-hour ISO 8601 format (date and time separated by a space, bracketed).  
   • Only one command per line.  
   • At least three log entries must reference a cut or paste command.  
   • The log file must end with a final newline character.

5. Leave the original source files untouched (they will be checked via checksum).

When you are finished, you should have:

• /home/user/datasets/summary_ab.tsv  
• /home/user/datasets/summary_c.tsv  
• /home/user/research_logs/column_extraction.log  

Nothing else in any other location needs to be created or modified.

The automated test system will verify:

1. That both summary files exist with the exact number of TAB-separated columns and with rows that match the source data character-for-character.  
2. That the log file exists, has the correct timestamped line format, contains at least three commands that include either “cut” or “paste”, and ends with a newline.  
3. That genes.tsv, expression.tsv and sample_info.tsv remain unchanged.

Perform the task entirely from the command line; do not copy-and-paste the expected output here—create the actual files on disk.
truth:
Initial files present at task start:

1. /home/user/datasets/genes.tsv  (exact contents)  
gene_id  gene_name  chromosome  
1  TP53  17  
2  EGFR  7  
3  BRCA1  17  
4  MYC  8  
5  MTOR  1  
(Fields separated by single TABs and each row terminated by \n.)

2. /home/user/datasets/expression.tsv  (exact contents)  
gene_id  sample_A  sample_B  sample_C  
1  12.5  13.0  11.8  
2  4.2  4.9  5.0  
3  7.7  8.1  7.5  
4  20.0  19.5  21.1  
5  10.3  9.8  10.0  

3. /home/user/datasets/sample_info.tsv  (exact contents)  
sample_id  tissue  condition  
sample_A  liver  treated  
sample_B  liver  control  
sample_C  heart  treated  

Files that must exist after the agent finishes (verification ground truth):

A) /home/user/datasets/summary_ab.tsv  
gene_id[tab]gene_name[tab]sample_A[tab]sample_B\n  
1[tab]TP53[tab]12.5[tab]13.0\n  
2[tab]EGFR[tab]4.2[tab]4.9\n  
3[tab]BRCA1[tab]7.7[tab]8.1\n  
4[tab]MYC[tab]20.0[tab]19.5\n  
5[tab]MTOR[tab]10.3[tab]9.8\n  

B) /home/user/datasets/summary_c.tsv  
gene_id[tab]gene_name[tab]sample_C\n  
1[tab]TP53[tab]11.8\n  
2[tab]EGFR[tab]5.0\n  
3[tab]BRCA1[tab]7.5\n  
4[tab]MYC[tab]21.1\n  
5[tab]MTOR[tab]10.0\n  

C) /home/user/research_logs/column_extraction.log  
• Plain UTF-8 text file.  
• Contains at least 5 lines.  
• Every line matches the regex  
  ^\[[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\] .+\n$  
• Must include at least three lines where the command substring matches /(cut|paste)/.  
• File ends with \n.

Permissions:  
– All existing dataset files are 644 (rw-r–r–).  
– /home/user owns everything.  
The agent’s shell runs as “user” and can create /home/user/research_logs and new files inside /home/user/datasets.

The automated test harness will re-compute SHA256 sums of summary_ab.tsv and summary_c.tsv and compare against these canonical strings (with TABs and trailing newlines). It will also validate the log rules above.
